hostExamples.title: Generic mcpServers JSON
hostExamples.whenToUse: your host supports HTTP MCP directly.
hostExamples.secretNote: Keep the developer token machine-local and never commit host config with inline credentials.

hostExamples.title: Codex remote setup
hostExamples.whenToUse: Codex should connect to the hosted UAT MCP endpoint directly.
hostExamples.secretNote: This writes a machine-local Codex config entry that contains the full query-token URL.

hostExamples.title: Codex npm bridge
hostExamples.whenToUse: Codex should launch a local stdio MCP bridge instead of remote HTTP MCP.
hostExamples.secretNote: Keep {{TOKEN_ENV_VAR}} local. The backend endpoint and token should not be committed.

hostExamples.title: npm bridge config
hostExamples.whenToUse: your host expects a local stdio process but supports generic mcpServers JSON.
hostExamples.secretNote: Keep {{TOKEN_ENV_VAR}} local. This should match the same endpoint and token you use for remote MCP.

hostExamples.title: Claude Desktop stdio
hostExamples.whenToUse: Claude Desktop is your MCP host and you need a local stdio bridge.
hostExamples.secretNote: Claude Desktop stores this config locally. Do not commit the token value.

hostExamples.title: Cursor / VS Code remote JSON
hostExamples.whenToUse: your editor host understands mcpServers JSON and can call remote MCP directly.
hostExamples.secretNote: The URL contains the token today, so keep the config file local.

hostExamples.title: Raw remote MCP URL
hostExamples.whenToUse: your host only asks for the MCP endpoint URL.
hostExamples.secretNote: Use the exact slash-safe mount shape.
